findings.rationale: Privilege escalation permits unauthorized teacher authority.
User-controlled metadata must never grant authorization.
Join-code disclosure can enable unauthorized enrollment attempts.
Offboarded users retaining access is an authorization failure.
Misleading invitation UX has a safe V1 enrollment alternative.
Archived classes can block a student's core workflow.
Incomplete confirmation can strand newly registered users.
Missing recovery can permanently block account access.
Conflicting enrollment decisions can create unauthorized membership.
Implicit workspace context risks cross-workspace access or mutation.
Silent caps hide students and enrollment requests from teachers.
Identity drift can break account lookup and administrative actions.
A retained code must not restore an offboarded user's access.
An overexposed Data API can bypass the intended security boundary.
Replayable worker kicks can amplify privileged global processing cost and availability impact.
Unbounded join-code attempts enable enrollment abuse and systematic code guessing.
Direct DML, sequence, or internal-schema access can bypass the reviewed RPC authorization boundary.
Incorrect join-code response handling can strand or mislead students during core enrollment.
A protected route must never show fixed demo feedback instead of the student's requested submission.
Browser-controlled maintenance operations can disrupt database availability and bypass the intended RPC-only boundary.
Direct writes can bypass feedback-mode authorization and release controls.
Private or uncertain feedback must never be exposed to students.
Immediate feedback is a core promised workflow.
Unretried failures can permanently lose evaluation outcomes.
Stale jobs can leave students indefinitely blocked.
Timeout mismatch can strand otherwise valid submissions.
Partial persistence can expose contradictory or corrupt feedback state.
Failed jobs must not leave student-visible private fragments.
Text corruption invalidates evaluator accuracy.
Whitespace and paragraph corruption invalidates evaluator accuracy.
Lost topics break feedback and adaptive practice.
Alias collisions can fail otherwise valid evaluations.
Incorrect diffs can misteach students.
Poison jobs threaten queue reliability for all users.
Weak semantic validation can release incorrect educational feedback.
Stale open pages can conceal ready feedback or failure states.
Incorrect success copy is misleading but does not corrupt data.
Post-limit filters can hide required teacher review work.
Writing attached to the wrong class is a context and privacy failure.
A stranded draft can lose student work.
Raw internal errors can disclose sensitive implementation details.
Unbounded or post-deadline provider bodies can exhaust worker resources and strand durable jobs.
Adversarial corrected text can trigger worst-case diff work and deny evaluator capacity.
Ambiguous response envelopes or model-role drift can make untrusted output appear authoritative.
Invalid or uncertain first-pass feedback must retry safely and remain private when confidence is unresolved.
A queue result alias mismatch makes the core writing submission transaction fail before durable acknowledgement.
A capped draft lookup can strand active writing behind an unrecoverable uniqueness conflict.
A restore-readiness race can strand valid student text outside revision-safe autosave.
Unwanted empty draft rows consume quota and can obstruct reliable draft history.
Generic revision-conflict failures prevent safe student recovery from concurrent writing or practice edits.
Terminologically false feedback can teach the wrong error concept even when the corrected span itself is accurate.
Recurring weaknesses require a usable active practice path.
Stale generation can leave practice indefinitely blocked.
Stale evaluation can leave an attempt indefinitely blocked.
Partial finalization can corrupt score and review state.
Rejecting valid answers directly harms educational correctness.
Exact scoring is unsafe for flexible language answers.
Destructive re-import can corrupt historical student attempts.
Partial imports can leave irreconcilable content revisions.
Lost mappings prevent the promised adaptive workflow.
Resolved weaknesses must not be immediately reopened by stale evidence.
Contradictory points and status invalidate student scores.
Contract mismatch can create unevaluable worksheets.
A sentinel is not a valid semantic-scoring contract.
Student personal data must not be sent unnecessarily to providers.
Unreviewed generated content can expose students to ambiguous work.
Relabeled fallback content violates CEFR accuracy.
Refresh must not lose a student's worksheet answers.
Teacher-support recommendations need an actionable resolution path.
Broken documentation blocks operators but not the runtime product.
Stale empty-state copy is misleading but recoverable.
A displayed but unreachable state breaks the adaptive contract.
Contradictory generation state can block usable practice.
Unattributed or unauthorized approval can make unsafe content reusable.
Answer prompt injection can corrupt educational scores and feedback.
Unreconciled production content can bypass the qualified worksheet bank.
A broken adaptive retry chain can strand a student without the required practice assignment.
Serial AI stages can exhaust one worker deadline and strand otherwise valid worksheet generation.
A hidden timeout clamp makes the durable worksheet recovery contract ineffective.
Accounting outside the stage budget can overrun workers and accept provider output after the safe deadline.
Conflicting checkpoint lock order can deadlock generation and cleanup.
A missing checkpoint-evidence index can turn cleanup and recovery into a launch-scale availability risk.
Blocking exact fallback or repair continuations after attempt three can strand a recoverable worksheet.
Ordinary retry backoff adds avoidable delay to an already-durable worksheet continuation.
An undersized Gemini critic deadline caused a real paid worksheet path to fail despite a healthy response.
Factually wrong teaching rules or contradictory task instructions can misteach students even when answer scoring remains structurally valid.
Lowercasing strict-case choices can reject valid capitalization and spelling practice before students receive material.
Passing a rejection wrapper instead of its candidate prevents the durable worksheet repair stage from starting.
Persisting a repair candidate with the wrong attempt identity breaks slot-two recovery and can strand generation.
A rejected generation with no exact certified fallback leaves the student without the core promised practice material.
Predictable answer positions undermine the educational value of the launch worksheet bank and let students bypass the target grammar.
Material backed by a revoked certifier or releaser must not remain available for untouched student practice.
Queue, authorization, or lock-order races that hide certified material can deny the core promised practice workflow.
Stale or exhausted adaptive transitions can corrupt mastery state or permanently strand a valid teacher override.
A contradictory or unscoped critic verdict can falsely reject valid generated material and deny the core practice workflow.
Skipping safe bank and cache rescue after a returned rejection can leave a student without practice material.
Teachers must complete the core workflow without assistance.
Viewport-inaccessible controls can block class configuration.
Unnamed controls block assistive-technology users.
Slow duplicate fetching threatens the launch performance gates.
Undocumented build requirements can prevent a reproducible release.
Missing automated regression protection makes launch unsafe.
The exposed network extension expands the database attack surface.
Leaked-password protection is mandatory for launch authentication.
A teacher default can become a privilege-escalation path.
Unsafe error formatting can leak details and block recovery.
Broad cleanup matching can destroy unrelated production data.
Deploying against the wrong project can corrupt or expose production data.
Demo or public teacher signup can bypass production controls.
Unverified monitoring and recovery conceal launch-critical failures.
Arbitrary monitoring routing can exfiltrate production credentials.
Obsolete network bootstrap can restore an unsafe production architecture.
Replayed operational evidence can falsely certify an unsafe release.
Self-reported finding counts cannot prove release readiness.
Unmeasured database work can violate launch performance targets.
Unit tests cannot prove the authenticated cross-role workflow works end to end.
Launch security requires current independent whole-system review evidence.
Unapproved minor data handling and external transfer create material privacy risk.
Authenticated test logs must not persist raw exceptions or request identifiers that can contain private data.
Broken database gates can hide release drift or fail before validating the intended security boundary.
Missing class and workspace controls can block core student workflows at common viewport widths.
A staging-only provider endpoint in production rollback artifacts can restore an unapproved privileged surface.
Incorrect browser-test discovery can create false failures or silently skip the configuration that a flow requires.
An unguarded configuration push can break production authentication redirects and recovery links.
Incorrect rate-limit translation can turn expected quota outcomes into failures or mislead students about unknown errors.
An unbounded spend RPC can indefinitely occupy writing and worksheet workers.
A live canary must never mutate arbitrary non-synthetic teacher or student resources.
Cleanup using a stale queue pointer can delete or retain the wrong durable work item.
Canary cleanup must not erase real provider spend from global hard-cap accounting.
A crash without a recovery manifest can leave persistent staging fixtures and invalidate later canaries.
A cleanup race can leave phantom active jobs after a canary falsely reports success.
A symlinked manifest parent can redirect writes or cleanup outside the owned workspace.
Opaque service keys in Bearer authorization can stop every durable AI worker and recovery path.
Without a verified operator-controlled onboarding journey, legitimate teachers cannot reliably enter V1 and disabled teachers may retain live authority.
An administrator password compromise or AAL1 session can grant cross-workspace privileged access and high-impact mutations.
A protected-route hook-order crash can block authenticated teacher navigation even though it does not weaken authorization.
A terminal job with permanently reserved AI spend can exhaust budgets and block reliable worker or fixture recovery.
A failed synthetic worksheet cleanup can invalidate repeatability and leave staging fixtures without implying a production data-path failure.
